Name: task_aware_rate_distortion_compression
Title: Task-Aware Rate-Distortion Compression: Aligning Model Compression with Task-Relevant Information
Short Hypothesis: By explicitly incorporating task-specific information into the rate-distortion framework, we can achieve more efficient compression and better generalization performance.
Related Work: Prior work in model compression has largely focused on generic rate-distortion trade-offs (Isik et al., 2021; Bu et al., 2021) without explicitly considering task-specific information. Recent advances in information bottleneck theory (Tishby et al., 2015) suggest that preserving task-relevant information is crucial for generalization. Our proposal bridges these two areas by introducing a task-aware distortion measure and aligning compression with the information bottleneck principle.
Abstract: Model compression is essential for deploying deep learning models in resource-constrained environments. While rate-distortion theory provides a principled framework for balancing compression ratio and model performance, existing methods often fail to consider task-specific information, leading to suboptimal compression strategies. We propose a task-aware rate-distortion framework that explicitly incorporates task-relevant information into the compression process. Our approach introduces a task-aware distortion measure that quantifies the impact of compression on task performance and aligns the compression strategy with the information bottleneck principle. Experiments on image classification and natural language processing tasks demonstrate that our method achieves superior compression efficiency and generalization performance compared to existing approaches. Our results highlight the importance of task-specific information in model compression and provide a new direction for designing efficient compression algorithms.
Experiments: Evaluate the proposed task-aware distortion measure on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet classification tasks, comparing it to standard MSE-based distortion measures.
Implement an adaptive compression strategy that dynamically allocates bits based on task-specific information content, and compare its performance to uniform compression strategies.
Measure generalization performance on out-of-distribution datasets to assess the robustness of the compressed models.
Quantify the trade-off between compression ratio and task performance using rate-distortion curves, and compare our method to state-of-the-art compression techniques.
Conduct ablations to isolate the impact of the task-aware distortion measure and the information bottleneck alignment.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The task-aware distortion measure may require task-specific tuning, limiting its generalizability across different tasks.
Computing task-relevant information may introduce additional computational overhead during compression.
The proposed method may not perform well on tasks where task-specific information is difficult to quantify.
The effectiveness of the approach may depend on the quality of the task-specific information estimation.
